name: 1. Labrador Retriever
fact1: Highly energetic and prone to weight gain; requires strict portion control and 1-2 hours of daily exercise.
fact2: Highly social and eager to please; thrives on companionship and can develop destructive behaviors if isolated.
young: Requires rigorous chew-training and socialization to prevent destructive chewing habits and jumping.
mid: Peak physical condition; ideal for dog sports, running, and swimming to maintain cardiovascular health.
old: Highly susceptible to joint issues like hip dysplasia; orthopedic beds and joint supplements (glucosamine) are recommended.

name: 2. German Shepherd
fact1: Exceptionally intelligent working dogs; need continuous mental stimulation (training, puzzles) to prevent anxiety.
fact2: Prone to hip dysplasia and bloat (gastric dilatation-volvulus); use slow-feeder bowls and avoid exercise right after meals.
young: Demands consistent, firm, and positive obedience training early on to manage their protective instincts.
mid: Needs a 'job' or advanced training activities (agility, tracking) to stay mentally and physically fulfilled.
old: Monitor closely for hind-leg weakness and degenerative myelopathy; keep them on single-level living if mobility decreases.

name: 3. Golden Retriever
fact1: Heavy shedders with a dense double coat; require brushing several times a week to prevent matting and manage shedding.
fact2: Prone to separation anxiety; they form intense bonds with their families and shouldn't be left alone for long periods.
young: Naturally 'mouthy' retrievers; provide plenty of appropriate chew toys and practice bite inhibition.
mid: Needs steady, daily cardiovascular exercise to prevent obesity, which can exacerbate joint problems.
old: High risk for cancers (like hemangiosarcoma and lymphoma); requires bi-annual veterinary wellness and cancer screenings.

name: 4. French Bulldog
fact1: Brachycephalic (flat-faced) breed; extreme caution is required in hot or humid weather as they cannot pant efficiently to cool down.
fact2: Prone to spinal issues (IVDD); they should be discouraged from jumping off furniture.
young: Avoid stairs and heavy impact exercises during growth to protect their developing spine and joints.
mid: Maintain strict weight management; even a little extra weight severely restricts their already compromised airways.
old: Breathing issues may worsen with age; provide soft food if dental issues arise and keep their environment stress-free.

name: 5. Poodle (Standard)
fact1: Hypoallergenic hair instead of fur; requires professional grooming and clipping every 4-6 weeks to prevent severe matting.
fact2: Extremely intelligent and sensitive; responds best to positive reinforcement and can become stressed in chaotic environments.
young: Highly impressionable; early socialization is key to preventing them from becoming high-strung or timid.
mid: Excellent stamina and athleticism; requires vigorous daily exercise, including running or swimming.
old: Prone to vision loss (cataracts/PRA) and Addison's disease; maintain consistent home layouts to help failing vision.

name: 6. Beagle
fact1: Driven entirely by their nose; must be kept on a leash or in a securely fenced area to prevent them from wandering off.
fact2: Very vocal breed (baying/howling); requires training and attention to manage noise levels.
young: Needs scent-based games to channel their tracking instincts productively and tire them out.
mid: Highly prone to obesity; strictly measure their food and avoid giving in to their begging behavior.
old: Prone to intervertebral disc disease and arthritis; provide ramps for furniture access and soft bedding.

name: 7. Bulldog (English)
fact1: Requires daily cleaning of their facial wrinkles with a damp cloth to prevent skin fold dermatitis and infections.
fact2: Extremely heat-sensitive and poor swimmers; must be supervised around water and kept in air-conditioning during summer.
young: Can be stubborn; requires short, engaging training sessions with high-value rewards.
mid: Low-energy breed, but still requires short, daily walks (15-20 mins) to prevent severe obesity.
old: High risk for severe arthritis and respiratory decline; requires a high-quality joint supplement and orthopedic support.

name: 8. Rottweiler
fact1: Powerful and fiercely loyal; requires extensive socialization with people and other animals to prevent over-protectiveness.
fact2: Prone to bone cancer and joint issues; keep them lean to reduce stress on their skeletal system.
young: Needs strong leadership and consistent boundary-setting before they reach their full, powerful adult size.
mid: Requires a mix of physical exercise and mental tasks (like obedience or weight-pulling sports) to stay balanced.
old: Monitor for signs of heart issues (subaortic stenosis) and cancer; provide a highly digestible senior diet.

name: 9. Dachshund
fact1: Elongated spine makes them highly susceptible to Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD); strictly prohibit jumping off beds/couches.
fact2: Strong prey drive; they love to dig and chase small animals, so yards must be secure.
young: Train them to use ramps for furniture early on so it becomes a lifelong, spine-saving habit.
mid: Weight management is absolutely critical; excess weight places catastrophic strain on their long backs.
old: Watch for sudden signs of paralysis or back pain; regular dental cleanings are required as they are prone to severe tooth decay.

name: 10. Yorkshire Terrier
fact1: Their fine, human-like hair requires daily brushing or a short 'puppy cut' to prevent painful mats.
fact2: Prone to collapsing trachea; always use a harness instead of a neck collar when walking them.
young: Can be difficult to house-train; requires extreme consistency and positive reinforcement.
mid: High energy despite their size; regular interactive play and short walks are necessary to curb excessive barking.
old: High risk for severe dental disease, which can lead to organ failure; prioritize soft foods and regular vet dentals.

name: 11. Boxer
fact1: A high-energy, playful breed that takes longer to mature (acts like a puppy until age 3); needs plenty of vigorous exercise.
fact2: Predisposed to certain cancers (mast cell tumors, lymphoma) and heart conditions (cardiomyopathy).
young: Can be overly exuberant and jumpy; focus heavily on 'four on the floor' training to prevent them from knocking people over.
mid: Thrives on daily runs or intense fetch sessions; mental stimulation is needed to prevent destructive chewing.
old: Requires close monitoring for lumps and bumps; keep them comfortable in extreme temperatures as they are somewhat brachycephalic.

name: 12. Siberian Husky
fact1: Notorious escape artists; require highly secure fencing that goes deep into the ground to prevent digging out.
fact2: Thick double coat blows out twice a year; never shave a Husky, as their coat regulates both heat and cold.
young: High prey drive and strong pack instincts; start recall training early, though they should rarely be off-leash.
mid: Bred to run all day; requires intense daily exercise (like pulling, running, or hiking) to prevent howling and destruction.
old: Prone to eye issues like cataracts and progressive retinal atrophy; keep their environment predictable.

name: 13. Doberman Pinscher
fact1: Extremely loyal 'velcro dogs' that must live indoors with their family; they have low tolerance for cold weather.
fact2: Highly prone to Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM); regular echocardiograms are recommended.
young: Requires intensive, positive socialization to ensure they grow into confident, rather than fearful or aggressive, adults.
mid: Highly athletic and intelligent; excels in advanced obedience, protection sports, and agility.
old: Keep them warm during winter with coats; monitor heart health and mobility issues closely.

name: 14. Great Dane
fact1: Giant breed with a short lifespan (7-10 years); highly prone to fatal bloat, requiring smaller meals and possibly a preventative stomach tack.
fact2: Takes up a lot of space but has low to moderate exercise needs; they are generally 'couch potatoes'.
young: Must be fed large-breed specific puppy food to ensure slow, steady growth and prevent severe bone/joint disorders.
mid: Needs a soft, large bed to prevent hygromas (fluid-filled swellings) on their elbows and joints.
old: Often experiences severe osteoarthritis and hind-end weakness; require ramps for vehicles and thick orthopedic bedding.

name: 15. Shih Tzu
fact1: Their luxurious coat requires daily grooming or regular clipping; their eyes must be kept clean to prevent tear staining and infections.
fact2: Bred primarily as companions; they do not tolerate isolation well and require moderate indoor play.
young: Requires early grooming desensitization so they tolerate brushing and clipping throughout their lives.
mid: Prone to dental issues and weight gain; monitor treats and maintain a consistent tooth-brushing routine.
old: Prone to corneal ulcers and dry eye; requires regular veterinary eye drops and gentle handling of their back.

name: 16. Australian Shepherd
fact1: Incredibly high drive and intelligence; requires a 'job' (herding, agility, trick training) or they will become highly destructive.
fact2: Prone to the MDR1 gene mutation, making them fatally sensitive to certain common veterinary medications (like Ivermectin).
young: Will often try to 'herd' children and pets by nipping at heels; redirect this behavior instantly.
mid: Requires 1-2 hours of intense physical and mental exercise daily; a simple walk is not enough.
old: Prone to hip dysplasia and vision issues (cataracts); transition to low-impact exercise like swimming.

name: 17. Chihuahua
fact1: Prone to hypoglycemia (low blood sugar), especially when young or stressed; requires small, frequent meals.
fact2: Often suffer from 'small dog syndrome' if not trained properly; they need the same rules and boundaries as large dogs.
young: Extremely fragile; must be handled with care to avoid drops, and socialization is critical to prevent fear-aggression.
mid: Prone to luxating patellas (slipping kneecaps); avoid letting them jump off high furniture.
old: Highly prone to severe periodontal disease; requires soft diets and regular veterinary dental scaling.

name: 18. Pug
fact1: Another heavily brachycephalic breed; highly susceptible to heatstroke and respiratory distress.
fact2: Prone to obesity, which drastically worsens their breathing and joint issues; strict calorie counting is required.
young: Keep the folds on their face clean and dry from a young age to prevent yeast and bacterial infections.
mid: Requires gentle, low-impact exercise during cool parts of the day to keep their weight managed.
old: Susceptible to Pug Dog Encephalitis (PDE) and severe arthritis; prioritize extreme comfort and a stress-free environment.

name: 19. Border Collie
fact1: Considered the most intelligent dog breed; their need for mental stimulation is unmatched, requiring complex daily tasks.
fact2: Highly sensitive to motion and sound; they may try to chase cars, bikes, or running children.
young: Focus heavily on impulse control and an 'off switch' so they learn how to relax indoors.
mid: Requires intense daily work (agility, flyball, herding); physical exhaustion alone won't tire them without mental puzzles.
old: Keep their minds active with scent games and puzzle toys even when their bodies slow down from arthritis.

name: 20. Pembroke Welsh Corgi
fact1: Achondroplastic (dwarf) breed; their long backs and short legs make them prone to spinal injuries (IVDD).
fact2: Bred to herd cattle; they are vocal, alert, and prone to nipping at heels if not trained properly.
young: Requires early obedience training to manage their bossy, independent herding dog nature.
mid: Keep them extremely trim; excess weight puts terrible stress on their long backs and short joints.
old: Prone to Degenerative Myelopathy (a progressive spinal cord disease); monitor their gait and provide mobility aids if needed.
